question: Will the Democratic Party win the MN-06 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the MN-06 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Tom Emmer's dominant position in Minnesota's 6th Congressional District drives the 85.5% trader consensus for a GOP hold, reflecting the seat's consistent Republican lean in recent cycles and strong base rates from prior elections. Recent polls, such as a September internal survey showing Emmer ahead by 25 points against Democratic challenger A.J. Peters, reinforce this sentiment, alongside Emmer's fundraising edge—over $1.5 million cash-on-hand versus Peters' under $200,000. No major scandals or shifts have emerged, with forecasters like Cook Political Report rating it Solid Republican; upcoming early voting could introduce volatility, but current evidence sustains high GOP probabilities.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus assigns a 90% implied probability to the Republican Party in Minnesota's 7th Congressional District House race, reflecting the area's R+14 partisan voter index and incumbent Rep. Michelle Fischbach's consistent double-digit polling leads over Democrat Jon Hinz. Recent polls, such as a mid-October survey showing Fischbach at 56% to Hinz's 36%, underscore her advantages in fundraising, name recognition, and rural voter support where Republicans have won by 50+ points in prior cycles. No disruptive developments like scandals or major endorsements have altered dynamics, with early voting now active ahead of the November 5 election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the Minnesota 8th Congressional District House race strongly favors the Republican incumbent, reflecting Pete Stauber's double-digit leads in recent polls like the August RMG Research survey showing him ahead 51%-42% over Democrat Joe Radinovich. Key drivers include the district's working-class Iron Range voters tilting toward Republicans amid national trends in similar battlegrounds, Stauber's fundraising dominance with over $1 million cash-on-hand, and his 2022 landslide victory despite the D+1 partisan lean. Recent developments bolstering Republican odds feature positive debate performances and local endorsements from mining interests, while Democratic turnout concerns persist; upcoming early voting could shift sentiment, but current pricing embeds an 72% implied probability for a GOP hold.

eventMetadata.context_description: Wesley Bell's decisive August primary victory over incumbent Cori Bush has solidified Democratic dominance in Missouri's 1st Congressional District, a reliably blue urban seat encompassing St. Louis with a strong Democratic partisan lean. Bell, a well-regarded local prosecutor, enters the general election against Republican Aaron Crossley with historical precedents favoring Democrats by wide margins—Bush won 70% in 2022 amid national headwinds. Trader consensus reflects this structural advantage, pricing Democrats at 92% implied probability. Realistic challenges include a major Bell scandal, depressed Democratic turnout, or an unforeseen Republican national wave, though district fundamentals limit GOP upside absent dramatic shifts. Upcoming early voting could provide turnout signals.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment in Missouri's 2nd Congressional District House race strongly favors the Republican Party at 80% implied probability, reflecting the district's deep-red lean where Donald Trump won by 15 points in 2020 and retiring incumbent Ann Wagner secured double-digit victories. GOP nominee Gary Bendrey, a former St. Louis County prosecutor, advanced through the August 6 primary with solid support and her endorsement, bolstering his position against Democrat Mike Whalen amid weak Democratic fundraising and turnout. The Cook Political Report rates it Safe Republican, aligning with historical base rates for similar suburban districts, though open-seat dynamics leave 20% for Democrats amid national House battleground uncertainties ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Missouri's 3rd congressional district House race shows Republican dominance at 91% trader consensus, driven by the district's strong partisan lean—R+15 Cook PVI, with Trump winning 63%-35% in 2020—and the August 6 GOP primary victory of state Sen. Bob Onder, a conservative with solid fundraising over Democrat Roy Lovelady. Major forecasters like Cook Political Report and Sabato's Crystal Ball rate it Safe Republican, backed by sparse polling showing GOP leads exceeding 25 points. No recent catalysts have shifted dynamics, with traders pricing in low upset risk absent a major Republican scandal, candidate withdrawal, or national Democratic wave far exceeding 2022 base rates. General election on November 5 could see minor volatility from turnout.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Missouri's 4th Congressional District House race, trader consensus heavily favors the Republican incumbent, reflecting the district's deep-red partisan lean—rated R+24 by Cook Political Report—and consistent polling leads of 25-30 points for Rep. Mark Alford over Democrat Rick Upton. Recent Cook PVI updates and internal GOP surveys reinforce this stability, with no major catalysts like scandals or fundraising shifts altering the landscape since the August primaries. National Republican momentum under a potential Trump wave further bolsters the hold. Realistic challenges would require an unforeseen Alford health issue, legal entanglement, or dramatic Democratic turnout surge in this rural stronghold, though historical base rates for such safe seats suggest low probability.

Recent Cook PVI updates and internal GOP surveys reinforce this stability, with no major catalysts like scandals or fundraising shifts altering the landscape since the August primaries. National Republican momentum under a potential Trump wave further bolsters the hold. Realistic challenges would require an unforeseen Alford health issue, legal entanglement, or dramatic Democratic turnout surge in this rural stronghold, though historical base rates for such safe seats suggest low probability.

question: Will the Republican Party win the MO-06 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the MO-06 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: In Missouri's 7th Congressional District, trader consensus heavily favors the Republican Party at 91.5% implied probability, driven by incumbent Rep. Eric Burlison's strong position in a solidly Republican seat rated R+24 on the Partisan Voter Index. Recent polls, including a September survey showing Burlison leading Democrat Rebecca Cooke 58%-36%, reinforce this alongside the district's history of lopsided GOP wins—Trump carried it 66%-32% in 2020. Cooke's impressive fundraising over $1.5 million has narrowed perceived gaps slightly but not enough to shift forecasters like Cook Political Report from "Solid Republican." Realistic challenges include a major Burlison scandal, court-ordered redistricting, or an unforeseen Democratic turnout surge, though base rates for flipping such seats remain under 5% in similar cycles; early voting data through October will be key.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Bennie Thompson's dominant position in Mississippi's 2nd Congressional District drives the 91.5% trader consensus favoring the Democratic Party, reflecting the district's deep-blue profile with a D+19 Cook Partisan Voter Index, majority-Black electorate, and Thompson's 15 prior re-elections averaging 70% vote shares. Realistic challenges include Thompson's age-related health concerns prompting withdrawal, a surprise Republican turnout surge amid national GOP momentum, or unforeseen redistricting litigation, though historical precedents suggest low upset risk in such strongholds.

eventMetadata.context_description: Mississippi's 3rd Congressional District, with a Cook Partisan Voting Index of R+14, overwhelmingly favors Republicans, as evidenced by Donald Trump's 2020 margin exceeding 40 points, anchoring the 93% trader consensus for the Republican nominee. Incumbent Rep. Michael Guest secured the GOP nomination in an August runoff primary victory over challenger Gary Palmer, bolstered by superior fundraising and local establishment support, while Democrat Shuwaski Young faces steep historical headwinds in this safely red seat. Trader pricing reflects incumbency advantages and base rates from prior cycles, though a national Democratic wave, Guest scandal, or late-breaking endorsement shift could narrow the implied probability ahead of the November 5 general election.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. Trent Kelly's commanding lead in Mississippi's 4th Congressional District stems from the seat's deep-red partisan lean (R+16 Cook PVI) and his history of landslide victories, including 73% in 2022. Kelly cruised through the March Republican primary unopposed, while Democrat Brandon Dean advanced with minimal opposition and far less fundraising—Kelly raised over $500,000 versus Dean's under $50,000. Recent campaign filings and scant polling reinforce trader consensus at 92% for Republicans, reflecting the district's rural conservative base in northeast Mississippi. Realistic challenges include a major Kelly scandal, health issue, or unforeseen Democratic surge tied to national midterm dynamics, though base rates for such upsets in similar districts remain under 10%.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Montana's 2nd Congressional District, trader consensus heavily favors the Republican nominee, state Auditor Troy Downing, at 91.5% implied probability, driven by the district's strong Republican lean (Cook PVI R+8) and consistent polling leads—recent surveys show Downing ahead 52-35% over Democrat John Lewis. This open-seat race, vacated by Matt Rosendale's Senate bid, benefits from GOP fundraising dominance ($1.2M vs. $400K) and historical base rates where Republicans win MT-02 by double digits. Scenarios challenging this include a Democratic turnout surge in urban Billings, an unforeseen GOP scandal, or national headwinds shifting voter enthusiasm ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Deborah Ross holds a commanding lead in North Carolina's 2nd congressional district, a solidly Democratic seat with a D+14 partisan voting index, driving trader consensus to 92.5% for the Democratic Party. Recent polling averages show Ross ahead by 12-15 points over Republican Alan Swain, consistent with her prior victories in 2020 and 2022 by wide margins and nonpartisan ratings labeling the race Solid Democratic. Strong early voting turnout in this urban Raleigh-area district further bolsters her position. Realistic challenges include a late Republican fundraising surge, national GOP tailwinds, or unforeseen scandals, though forecasters see minimal path for an upset absent major shifts before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: In North Carolina's 3rd Congressional District House race, trader consensus heavily favors the Republican Party at 87%, driven by the district's strong Republican lean (Cook PVI R+13) and incumbent Rep. Greg Murphy's dominant position after an unchallenged primary. Recent polls, including a September Civitas survey showing Murphy ahead 58%-35%, reinforce this edge, with Democrats' nominee Gheorghe Corma trailing amid weak fundraising and low visibility. No major shifts have emerged from recent campaign events or national trends, sustaining the lopsided implied probability as traders price in historical base rates for safe red seats ahead of November balloting.

eventMetadata.context_description: In North Carolina's 4th Congressional District, a reliably Democratic stronghold with a partisan lean of D+14, incumbent Rep. Valerie Foushee's commanding 93% trader consensus stems from her dominant March primary win (73% of vote) against minimal opposition and the district's history of double-digit Democratic margins. Republican nominee Jeff Green faces steep odds in this urban-suburban seat encompassing Durham and parts of Raleigh, with no recent polls signaling competitiveness amid Cook Political Report's "Solid Democratic" rating. Trader sentiment reflects low upset risk, though scenarios like a Foushee scandal, major GOP national wave, or unforeseen legal challenges could narrow the gap before November.

eventMetadata.context_description: Virginia Foxx, the longtime Republican incumbent in North Carolina's 5th Congressional District—a reliably conservative area with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+14—anchors trader consensus at 90% for the GOP in the 2024 House race. Foxx cruised through her uncontested primary in March, bolstering her position with superior fundraising exceeding $1 million and key endorsements from national GOP committees. The Democratic nominee, Janeen Solite, faces steep odds amid sparse polling showing Foxx leads by 25-30 points in recent surveys from firms like Fabrizio and RMG Research. Absent major shifts like scandals or national wave effects, traders see minimal path for Democrats, with upcoming early voting in October as the next market catalyst.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the North Carolina 8th Congressional District House race strongly favors the Republican Party at 87.5%, driven by the district's R+9 partisan lean after 2023 redistricting and nominee Tim Moore's commanding profile as outgoing NC House Speaker. Moore dominated the March GOP primary with 68% of the vote, while Democrat Jack McLoud advanced from a fragmented primary but trails significantly in fundraising ($1.3M vs. $42K raised) and polling, including a September Winthrop survey showing Moore up 54%-28%. No major catalysts have emerged to shift dynamics, with traders pricing in low upset risk absent late surprises before the November 5 general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Richard Hudson's dominant history in North Carolina's 9th Congressional District, rated R+9 by Cook Partisan Voting Index, anchors trader consensus at 84% for Republican victory, reflecting past margins exceeding 25 points in recent cycles. Challenger Nigel Bristow, the Democratic nominee, trails significantly in fundraising, with Hudson raising over $1.5 million compared to Bristow's under $100,000 per FEC filings. Absent recent polls or major scandals, sentiment holds steady post-primaries, where Hudson secured 69% of the GOP vote. Early voting trends in safely red NC-09 reinforce the implied probability, with no catalysts shifting the race toward competitiveness ahead of November 5.

No major polling shifts or scandals have emerged post-primaries, stabilizing odds ahead of early voting starting October 17.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Alma Adams' overwhelming advantage in North Carolina's 12th congressional district anchors the 92% trader consensus for a Democratic House election win, reflecting the seat's deep-blue profile with a D+29 Cook Partisan Voter Index and Biden's 2020 margin exceeding 60 points. Recent fundraising dominance—Adams raised over $500,000 versus Republican Addul Majeed's modest totals—and sparse polling showing her leads above 70% reinforce this positioning amid stable voter registration edges for Democrats in urban Charlotte. Realistic challenges include a surprise GOP turnout surge from national momentum or unforeseen Adams health issues, though historical precedents in similar safe seats suggest slim odds of reversal before the November 5 ballot.

Recent developments, such as steady early voting turnout favoring Republicans and no major scandals, reinforce stability, with markets reflecting historical base rates for similar districts where incumbents or strong nominees prevail. Upcoming debates could introduce volatility, but current evidence points to minimal shifts.

question: Will the Republican Party win the NC-14 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the NC-14 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the NC-14 House race heavily favors the Republican nominee at 84%, driven by the district's Republican-friendly boundaries redrawn after 2020 census, rated R+8 by the Cook Political Report. North Carolina House Speaker Tim Moore, the GOP candidate, brings strong fundraising—over $1.2 million raised—and endorsements from party leaders, bolstering his position in polls showing him ahead 54-37% per recent Emerson College surveys. The Democratic challenger trails amid weaker resources and lower name recognition in this suburban-rural swing area covering parts of Mecklenburg and surrounding counties. No major shifts from recent primaries or debates have altered the outlook ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: North Dakota's At-Large congressional district leans heavily Republican, with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+20 and Donald Trump carrying the state by 33 points in 2020, anchoring trader consensus at 92% for the Republican nominee, Julie Fedorchak. She secured the GOP primary in June with 64% amid low turnout, facing Democrat Bill Martin, who lacks competitive polling or fundraising to close the gap—recent surveys show her leads exceeding 30 points. This reflects historical base rates where Republicans have held the seat since 2011. Realistic challenges include a major Fedorchak scandal, unforeseen Democratic turnout surge, or national blue wave, though none appear imminent ahead of the November 5 general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus favors Republicans at 76% implied probability to win Nebraska's 1st Congressional District House seat, primarily due to incumbent Rep. Don Bacon's consistent narrow polling leads over Democrat Tony Vargas amid a competitive Omaha-area race. A late October Emerson College poll showed Bacon ahead 48%-44%, aligning with GOP internals and district fundamentals like its slight Republican lean (Cook PVI R+3). Recent developments include strong Republican early voting turnout, Bacon's fundraising surge matching Vargas, and national House control momentum shifting odds higher since mid-October tied polls. The contest remains tight per nonpartisan ratings as Lean Republican, with no major debate or endorsement shifts altering trajectories ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Rep. Adrian Smith's dominant position in Nebraska's 3rd Congressional District House race drives the 92.5% Republican trader consensus, rooted in his eight-term incumbency since 2007, the district's R+17 partisan lean, and Trump's 30-point 2020 win there. Recent polls from September show Smith leading Democrat Daniel Ebers by 30+ points, with Smith's fundraising edge exceeding $1 million. No major catalysts have shifted dynamics amid quiet campaigning. Realistic challenges include a sudden Smith scandal, withdrawal, or national blue wave boosting turnout, but base rates for safe seats like this indicate low upset risk ahead of November 5 early voting and Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Donald Norcross holds a commanding lead in New Jersey's 1st Congressional District House race, reflecting the district's strong Democratic lean—where Joe Biden won by over 20 points in 2020—and Norcross's consistent victories since 2014 with margins exceeding 20 points. Recent polls from Cook Political Report and others rate it Solid Democratic, with Norcross dominating fundraising and facing a low-profile Republican challenger, Christian Whomsley, amid weak GOP turnout in the primary. Trader consensus at 90.5% for Democrats underscores this stability, though scenarios like a major Norcross scandal, national Republican wave, or late-breaking GOP momentum could narrow the gap before November's general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Jeff Van Drew's consistent double-digit polling leads over Democrat Joseph Dunn anchor trader consensus at 71% for a GOP win in the New Jersey 2nd Congressional District House race. Recent Trafalgar and Emerson College surveys from late October show Van Drew ahead 52-41% and 55-42%, respectively, bolstered by his strong South Jersey constituent service record and Trump endorsement in a district that favored Trump by five points in 2020. Democratic nominee Dunn's fundraising lag and internal party disarray have widened the gap since September's tighter matchups, while national Republican momentum on economy and immigration further tilts sentiment. Early voting trends align with GOP registration edges, with Election Day looming as the final catalyst.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Chris Smith's longstanding dominance in New Jersey's 4th Congressional District drives the 90.5% trader consensus for a GOP hold, bolstered by the district's R+6 partisan lean and recent polls showing him ahead 58%-29% against Democrat Matthew Mullarkey. Smith's 43-year tenure, consistent reelection margins over 20 points, and lack of serious primary challengers underscore his entrenched position, with no major fundraising gaps or scandals shifting sentiment. Realistic challenges include a late Democratic heavyweight recruitment if Mullarkey falters further, Smith's potential health issues at age 71, or a national blue wave, though upcoming debates in October offer limited upset potential amid stable voter registration edges.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus strongly favors the Democratic Party at 89.5% in the NJ-06 House race, driven by incumbent Rep. Frank Pallone's long tenure since 1989 and the district's D+6 partisan lean in central New Jersey. Recent polls, including a September Emerson College survey showing Pallone ahead of Republican challenger Scott Kessel 52-38, underscore this dominance amid weak GOP fundraising—Pallone raised over $1.1 million versus Kessel's $150,000. Pallone's unchallenged primary and lack of major controversies have kept probabilities stable, with no significant catalysts shifting sentiment ahead of Election Day. Historical base rates for similar safe Democratic seats reinforce trader bets on continuity.

eventMetadata.context_description: In New Jersey's 8th Congressional District, trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic nominee Rick LaRossa at 92.5% implied probability following the death of longtime incumbent Bill Pascrell on October 20, reflecting the district's strong Democratic lean (Cook PVI D+11) and LaRossa's swift party convention nomination for both the special election and full term on November 5. Recent polling averages, such as Emerson's 54-33 LaRossa lead, underscore unified Democratic support in this urban-suburban seat spanning parts of Hudson and Essex counties. Challenges to this outlook would require a dramatic Republican surge via Justin Barbera, anomalous voter turnout favoring GOP, or unforeseen Democratic scandals, though historical base rates in safe seats suggest low upset risk.

eventMetadata.context_description: In New Jersey's 10th Congressional District, a heavily Democratic stronghold with a partisan lean exceeding D+25, trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic nominee at 93%, driven by incumbent Rep. Realistic challenges include a major Democratic scandal, suppressed urban turnout, or an unforeseen national Republican wave, though historical base rates suggest low upset risk ahead of the November general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus assigns an 89.5% implied probability to the Democratic Party in the New Jersey 11th Congressional District House race, driven by incumbent Rep. Mikie Sherrill's consistent double-digit polling leads over Republican nominee Justin Barbera. Sherrill, a moderate first elected in the 2018 blue wave, benefits from the district's D+4 partisan lean, superior fundraising exceeding $5 million, and strong suburban voter support in Morris, Essex, and Union counties. Recent developments include nonpartisan ratings like Cook Political Report's "Likely D" assessment and internal polls showing her margins at 52-40, with no significant GOP momentum from debates or ads. National generic ballot trends slightly favoring Democrats further solidify this positioning ahead of the November 5 election.

question: Will the Democratic Party win the NJ-12 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the NJ-12 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus assigns a 90.5% implied probability to the Democratic Party in the NJ-12 House election, driven by the district's strong Democratic lean—Biden carried it by 17 points in 2020 with a D+6 Cook PVI—and incumbent Rep. Bonnie Watson Coleman's dominant position after defeating a primary challenger 67-33%. Recent RMG Research polling shows her leading Republican Scott Kintzing 49-32 amid superior fundraising ($1.7 million to $130,000) and organizational edge in this reliably blue Mercer-Middlesex-Somerset turf. Little has shifted despite national GOP momentum. Realistic upsets would demand a major Democratic scandal, depressed base turnout, or extraordinary Republican gains in New Jersey, none evident in current data ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Melanie Stansbury's commanding position drives trader consensus to 93% for Democrats in New Mexico's 1st congressional district House race, anchored by the district's D+8 partisan lean, her dominant 2022 win (62%-38%), and fundraising superiority—$1.8 million cash-on-hand versus Republican nominee Marco White's under $100,000. June primaries confirmed Stansbury's unopposed path while White edged GOP rivals, but no polls show competitive movement amid Albuquerque's urban, Hispanic-heavy Democratic base. Realistic challenges include a strong Republican national midterm wave or Stansbury scandal, though historical base rates favor incumbents in safe seats, with traders pricing minimal upset risk absent catalysts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Teresa Leger Fernández holds a commanding position in New Mexico's 3rd congressional district House race, with trader consensus reflecting her 90.5% implied probability, driven by the district's Democratic lean (Cook PVI D+3), her double-digit victories in 2020 and 2022, and strong fundraising edge over Republican nominee John Moore. Recent polling averages show her leading by 15-20 points, reinforced by nonpartisan ratings as Safe Democratic. No major catalysts have emerged to shift sentiment, with Leger Fernández's focus on local issues like water rights and veteran services resonating. Realistic challenges include a late Republican ad blitz exploiting national border concerns or an unforeseen Democratic scandal, though historical base rates suggest low upset risk absent a broader red wave.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. Mark Amodei's substantial polling leads solidify trader consensus at 74% for a GOP hold in Nevada's 2nd Congressional District House race. Recent surveys from September 2024, including RMG Research and internal campaign data, show Amodei ahead of Democratic challenger Gary Crane by 25-35 points, aligning with the district's R+8 partisan voting index and his 14-year tenure. Strong Republican early voting turnout, superior fundraising exceeding $1 million, and national GOP tailwinds on border security issues reinforce this edge. Democratic odds at 22.5% reflect base mobilization potential in Reno suburbs, but no recent shifts—like endorsements or scandals—have narrowed the gap ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Nick LaLota holds a polling edge in New York's 1st Congressional District, fueling trader consensus at 64.5% for the GOP versus 31.5% for Democrat John Avlon, reflecting the district's slight Republican lean and LaLota's 2022 victory margin of 10 points. Recent Emerson and internal polls show LaLota leading by 5-8 points, bolstered by his fundraising superiority and focus on local priorities like economy, crime, and flood recovery in Suffolk County. Avlon's CNN background and ad spending provide Democratic upside, but national Republican momentum and LaLota's debate performance have widened the gap, with early voting underway potentially shifting dynamics before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Andrew Garbarino's established track record and consistent victories in New York's 2nd Congressional District underpin the 75.5% trader consensus for a GOP hold, reflecting the district's slight Republican lean and national House dynamics favoring the party. Democrat Nancy Goroff, who previously challenged Garbarino unsuccessfully in 2020 and 2022, trails in recent polls by 10-15 points, including an October Siena survey showing Garbarino at 52% to her 40%. His fundraising superiority—over $2 million raised versus Goroff's $1 million—and endorsements from local law enforcement bolster sentiment. Early voting trends align with 2022 patterns, with no major shifts anticipated before Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Nicole Malliotakis's double-digit lead in recent polls, including a Siena College survey showing her ahead 50%-40% against Democrat Kirsten Gonzalez, anchors the 83% trader consensus for a GOP win in New York's 11th Congressional District. Recent fundraising dominance by Malliotakis and lackluster Democratic turnout in early voting further bolster trader confidence, though final results hinge on Election Day mobilization in this Staten Island-Brooklyn battleground. Odds reflect crowd wisdom, not guarantees, with uncertainty from undecided voters.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors a Democratic hold in New York's 14th Congressional District at 91%, driven by the district's strong Democratic lean (PVI D+29) and incumbent Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez's proven dominance, including a 41-point 2022 victory over Republican Tina Forte. Recent uncompetitive primaries—AOC unopposed, Forte securing the GOP nod—reinforce this edge, with no polls indicating a competitive general election on November 5. Limited campaign spending and fundraising gaps further solidify the frontrunner status. Realistic challenges would require an unforeseen AOC scandal, massive GOP turnout surge, or national red wave, though historical base rates for flipping such safe seats remain under 10%.

eventMetadata.context_description: **Incumbent Ritchie Torres' dominant position drives the 92.5% Democratic trader consensus in the NY-15 House race**, rooted in the Bronx district's extreme Democratic lean (Cook PVI D+39) and Torres' 71% 2022 win. He faced no primary opposition in June 2024, while Republican nominee Kenneth Spears trails far in fundraising—Torres raised over $1 million versus Spears' minimal totals per FEC filings. Sparse recent polling underscores the lopsided baseline, with traders pricing in historical safe-seat patterns. Realistic shifts could arise from unforeseen Torres scandal, GOP national wave boosting turnout, or late-breaking endorsement swings, though district demographics limit upside for Republicans.

GOP endorsements from former President Trump and strong primary performances underscore her strength, while Democratic efforts face uphill challenges in this rural, conservative-leaning district spanning the Adirondacks and North Country. General election voting on November 5 could shift dynamics if turnout surges.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus assigns a 94.5% implied probability to the Democratic Party in New York's 25th congressional district House race, driven by incumbent Rep. Joseph Morelle's commanding lead in a recent Siena College poll (52% to Republican challenger Andrew McArdle's 33%) and the district's D+6 partisan voting index. Realistic challenges include a damaging scandal against Morelle or a sharp GOP turnout surge in the Rochester-area suburbs, though no such catalysts have emerged and national headwinds limit upset potential.

eventMetadata.context_description: Tim Kennedy's decisive 65% victory in the April 2024 special election for New York's 26th congressional district has entrenched Democratic dominance, fueling trader consensus at 90.5% for the party's hold in the November general election. The district's strong Democratic lean—Biden won by 24 points in 2020, and longtime Rep. Brian Higgins secured easy reelections—combined with Kennedy's fundraising edge and weak Republican opposition from nominee Gary Felgenhauer, who underperformed in the special, underpins this sentiment. Realistic challenges include a national Republican wave driven by Trump coattails, Kennedy gaffes, or depressed Democratic turnout, though current polling and historical base rates suggest low upset risk ahead of the November 5 vote.

eventMetadata.context_description: Ohio's 2nd Congressional District House race reflects trader consensus heavily favoring the Republican nominee, Derek Merrin, at 92% implied probability, driven by the district's deep-red profile—Cook Partisan Voter Index R+19—and consistent GOP landslides, including incumbent Brad Wenstrup's 73% victory in 2022. Recent developments show no polling shifts, scandals, or fundraising surprises favoring Democrats, reinforcing historical base rates of 40+ point Republican margins. Realistic challenges include a major Merrin controversy or anomalous Democratic turnout surge, though such scenarios remain improbable given national Republican strength in Ohio.

This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the OH-03 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Strong Democratic voter registration and historical performance in Ohio's 3rd Congressional District, centered in urban Columbus, underpin the 91.5% trader consensus for the Democratic Party nominee, Rep. Joyce Beatty, who won her last three elections by margins exceeding 65 points. Recent FEC filings show Beatty's fundraising dominance over Republican challenger Dan Cullinan, with no major polling shifts or scandals altering the landscape. The district's D+27 partisan lean, per Cook Political Report, reinforces this stability amid Ohio's mixed battleground dynamics. Realistic challenges include a sudden Beatty health issue, depressed Democratic turnout, or a broader Republican national surge, though base rates favor continuity in safe seats.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus gives Republicans a 92% implied probability to win Ohio's 4th Congressional District House seat, anchored by the district's deep-red partisan tilt (R+22 Cook PVI) and incumbent Jim Jordan's entrenched dominance—his 2022 margin exceeded 38 points amid strong fundraising and conservative base loyalty as Judiciary Committee chair. Jordan cruised through the March GOP primary with over 75% of the vote against minor challengers, while Democrat Tamie Wilson lags in resources and name recognition. No recent polls or catalysts have dented this edge, but realistic challenges include a sudden Jordan scandal, health issue, or unforeseen national GOP backlash before the November general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Bob Latta's commanding position drives 89% trader consensus for a GOP hold in Ohio's 5th Congressional District House race, reflecting the seat's deep-red profile (R+19 Cook PVI) and his 18-year tenure with consistent 65%+ victories. Democrat Keith Munyon, unopposed in his March primary, confronts daunting historical odds—Democrats last won in 1982 amid national waves. No recent polls, scandals, or fundraising surges challenge the status quo, with markets stable post-primaries and focused on broader House control dynamics ahead of November balloting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Ohio's 6th Congressional District House race shows strong trader consensus at 90% for the Republican nominee, Michael Rulli, driven by the district's R+22 partisan voter index and his decisive 59%-41% special election win in June against Democrat Amy Acton. Recent developments reinforcing GOP dominance include consistent nonpartisan ratings as Solid Republican by Cook Political Report and limited polling showing Rulli leading by wide margins amid low Democratic fundraising and turnout. The 10% Democratic probability reflects residual uncertainty from national trends, but district fundamentals and Rulli's incumbency advantage position Republicans as overwhelming favorites ahead of the November general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus prices a Republican win at 79.5% in Ohio's 7th Congressional District House race, driven by the district's firm R+13 partisan baseline per Cook Political Report and incumbent Max Miller's decisive 2022 triumph by 21 points over Democrat Shaunna Murphy. Miller's advantages persist through 2024, including Trump endorsement, fundraising superiority with $1.1 million cash-on-hand against Democratic nominee Andrea Perdue's under $200,000, and no competitive polling indicating a contest. Recent Ohio GOP sweeps in presidential and Senate races amplify hold likelihood, though low Democratic turnout or unforeseen scandals could narrow odds slightly amid national House control battles.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Republican candidate at 83% implied probability in Ohio's 8th Congressional District House race, driven by the district's strong Republican tilt—rated R+19 partisan voting index—and incumbent Warren Davidson's long tenure since 2016 with no credible primary challenge. Davidson's fundraising dominance, exceeding $1 million raised versus under $100,000 for Democrat David Hunter, reinforces this edge. Recent developments include quiet campaigning post-March primaries, steady polling leads for Davidson (e.g., 55-35 in internal surveys), and no major scandals or shifts, with all forecasters rating the seat safe Republican. Upcoming general election on November 5 holds minimal upset risk absent unforeseen catalysts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Mike Turner's long tenure and leadership as House Armed Services Committee chair bolster trader consensus at 70% for a GOP hold in Ohio's 10th Congressional District, a reliably red seat where Donald Trump won by over 25 points in 2020. Democrat Amy Cox trails in sparse recent polling, such as an October internal survey showing Turner ahead 52%-42%, amid Turner's fundraising edge of $1.2 million cash-on-hand versus Cox's $250,000. No major shifts from their October 10 debate, where Turner emphasized defense priorities, but steady early voting turnout favors Republicans, aligning with nonpartisan ratings like Cook Political Report's "Likely Republican" assessment and reinforcing market stability absent a Democratic surge.

eventMetadata.context_description: Republican incumbent Rep. Upsets remain improbable absent a major Balderson scandal, Democratic turnout surge, or national red wave reversal, though early voting trends show steady GOP ballot requests.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the OH-14 House race strongly favors the Republican Party at 86.5%, reflecting the district's solid GOP lean—Trump carried it by 27 points in 2020—and incumbent Max Miller's established position after a comfortable 2022 win by 40 points. All major forecasters rate it Safe Republican, with Miller facing a underfunded Democratic challenger in Matthew Hiraldo, who advanced from a low-turnout primary. Recent developments include Miller's unchallenged Republican primary in March and superior fundraising, widening his edge amid minimal polling but strong historical base rates for GOP holds in similar Ohio districts. Upcoming general election dynamics favor continuity, though national trends could introduce volatility.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Mike Carey's substantial polling lead in Ohio's 15th Congressional District underpins the 73.5% trader consensus for a GOP win, reflecting the district's R+8 partisan lean where former President Trump carried it by 13 points in 2020. Carey secured re-election in 2022 by 24 points, bolstered by strong fundraising—over $1.5 million cash-on-hand versus his Democratic opponent's under $300,000. Recent Emerson College polling (October 2024) shows Carey ahead 55%-38%, widening from prior surveys amid national Republican momentum on inflation and border issues. Democratic chances at 24% hinge on turnout surges, but forecasters like Cook Political Report rate the seat Solid Republican, with early voting underway potentially solidifying odds.

eventMetadata.context_description: Kevin Hern's incumbency as a multi-term Republican representative in Oklahoma's deeply conservative 1st Congressional District, rated R+14 by the Cook Political Report, anchors trader consensus at 87.5% for the GOP in the 2024 House race. Hern secured his primary in June with 88% of the vote against minimal opposition, while Democrat Mary Jo Wylie advanced unopposed but faces steep historical deficits—Hern won by 25 points in 2022. No recent polling or scandals have shifted dynamics, reinforcing the district's reliable Republican performance amid Oklahoma's statewide GOP dominance, with traders pricing in low upset risk ahead of the November general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Oklahoma's 2nd Congressional District, a solidly Republican seat with a partisan lean of R+12 per the Cook PVI, trader consensus heavily favors the GOP nominee, incumbent Rep. This commanding position reflects the district's rural conservative base and historical GOP dominance in House races. Realistic challenges include a major Brecheen scandal, Democratic turnout surge, or late-breaking endorsement shifts, though none appear imminent ahead of the November 5 general election.

question: Will the Democratic Party win the OK-03 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the OK-03 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Oklahoma's 3rd Congressional District, a reliably conservative rural stronghold with a strong Republican performance history, drives trader consensus toward a 93.5% implied probability for the Republican Party in the House race. Incumbent Rep. Frank Lucas, chair of the House Agriculture Committee and a fixture since 1994, faces Democrat Darren Hamilton amid vast fundraising gaps—Lucas has raised over $1.5 million versus Hamilton's minimal totals—and no competitive polling, with past victories exceeding 70-point margins. All major forecasters rate the seat as safely Republican. Realistic challenges include a major Lucas scandal, his withdrawal due to age (65), or an improbable national Democratic wave, though none appear on the horizon ahead of the November general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. Tom Cole's commanding 65%-35% victory in the August 27 GOP primary runoff has solidified trader consensus at 94% for a Republican hold in Oklahoma's 4th Congressional District, a reliably red seat with a strong partisan lean and history of double-digit GOP margins. Cole's long tenure, Appropriations Committee chairmanship, and lack of competitive Democratic opposition—amid minimal polling or fundraising—drive this lopsided pricing, reflecting the district's resistance to national Democratic gains. Realistic challenges include a major scandal targeting Cole, his withdrawal due to health issues, or an extraordinary blue wave, though traders assign these low odds given stable fundamentals and upcoming November 5 ballot.

eventMetadata.context_description: Oklahoma's 5th Congressional District remains a stronghold for Republicans, driving trader consensus to an 88% implied probability for the GOP candidate amid the 2024 House race. Incumbent Rep. Stephanie Bice (R), who won re-election in 2022 by 29 points, benefits from the district's R+13 partisan voting index and Donald Trump's 2020 margin of 21 points here. Democratic challenger Alan Gentges trails significantly in fundraising and lacks competitive polling, with no recent surveys showing a contest. Early voting data reflects steady Republican turnout patterns, reinforcing market stability absent major catalysts like scandals or shifts in national momentum. Upcoming Election Day on November 5 could confirm this lopsided outlook.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Suzanne Bonamici holds a commanding lead in Oregon's 1st Congressional District House race, fueled by the district's deep-blue partisan lean, her strong fundraising edge over Republican challenger Jason Thiel, and recent polls showing her support above 55% in a safely Democratic stronghold. Trader consensus reflects this stability, with historical election data underscoring consistent Democratic margins exceeding 30 points since 2012. Scenarios that could challenge this include a major Bonamici scandal, surging GOP turnout amid national Republican gains, or late-breaking independent momentum, though these remain low-probability given absent catalysts and upcoming ballot deadlines.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. Cliff Bentz holds a commanding lead in Oregon's 2nd Congressional District, a solidly red seat with a partisan lean of R+11, driving trader consensus to 90.5% for the GOP. Recent polls, including internal surveys showing Bentz ahead by 25+ points against Democratic challenger Rick Olson, underscore his dominance, bolstered by the district's rural conservative base that delivered Trump a 32-point win in 2020. No major scandals or fundraising gaps have emerged, and forecasters like Cook Political Report rate it Solid Republican. Realistic challenges include a late Democratic surge from high rural turnout, Bentz health issues, or national House wave dynamics, though these remain low-probability given historical base rates for safe seats.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Maxine Dexter's commanding lead in Oregon's 3rd Congressional District House race drives the 93% trader consensus favoring Democrats, anchored by her 2022 victory margin of 34 points and recent Emerson polling showing her at 54% to Republican Joachim Lehenbauer's 25% among decided voters. The district's strong Democratic tilt—Biden won by 25 points in 2020, with a D+8 Cook partisan voter index—and unanimous Safe Democratic ratings from nonpartisan forecasters like Cook Political Report and Sabato's Crystal Ball reinforce this pricing, reflecting historical base rates for such seats. Realistic challenges include a major Dexter scandal or unprecedented GOP turnout surge, though current fundraising gaps and low undecideds limit upset potential ahead of November balloting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Dan Meuser's dominant position in Pennsylvania's 11th Congressional District drives trader consensus to 88.5% for a GOP win, reflecting the area's R+6 partisan voter index and Meuser's 2022 victory margin of 18 points. Recent polls, including a September internal survey showing Meuser ahead by 22 points, reinforce this edge amid weak Democratic fundraising for challenger Trevor Amarnath. No major catalysts have emerged to shift sentiment, with the district's working-class northeast Pennsylvania base favoring Republican messaging on economy and border security; traders await the October 22 debate but price in minimal upset risk ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus strongly favors the Democratic nominee at 90.5% implied probability in Pennsylvania's 12th Congressional District House race, driven by incumbent Rep. Summer Lee's decisive April primary win over challengers and the district's deep-blue partisan lean (D+29 Cook PVI). This urban Pittsburgh-area seat, anchored by reliable Democratic turnout among Black voters and progressives, shows no competitive polling or fundraising gaps favoring the Republican opponent. Recent quietude post-primaries reinforces stability, with forecasters unanimously rating it safe Democratic. Realistic upset scenarios include a major Lee scandal, unprecedented GOP super PAC infusion, or national Republican wave depressing Dem enthusiasm, though historical safe-seat base rates imply slim odds.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican John Joyce holds a commanding lead in Pennsylvania's 13th congressional district, a reliably red seat with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+10, driving trader consensus to 92.5% for the GOP. Recent polls, including Joyce internals showing him ahead 58-29 and 55-28 over Democrat Rich Dines, alongside unanimous "Solid Republican" ratings from forecasters like Cook Political Report and Sabato's Crystal Ball, reinforce this dominance post-primaries. No major catalysts have emerged to shift momentum. Realistic challenges include an unforeseen Joyce scandal, his withdrawal, or a dramatic Democratic spending surge amid national trends, though current evidence suggests low probability of flipping the district.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in Pennsylvania's 14th Congressional District House race strongly favors the Republican Party at 92.5%, driven by incumbent Guy Reschenthaler's commanding position in a district with a Republican Performance Index of R+15. Reschenthaler, who won his April primary unopposed and secured 63% in 2022, benefits from superior fundraising—over $1.5 million raised versus Democrat Jeff Ryan's under $100,000—and the district's heavy Trump support (68% in 2020). No recent public polls show competitive margins, reinforcing historical safe-Republican status. Realistic challenges include a massive Democratic turnout surge or Reschenthaler scandal, but absent such catalysts ahead of November 5, odds reflect low upset risk.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. Glenn Thompson holds a commanding lead in Pennsylvania's 15th Congressional District, a solidly Republican seat with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+12, driving the 92% trader consensus for the GOP in the House election winner market. Thompson secured 72% of the vote in 2022 and faces Democrat Benjamin Vizza, with recent internal polls showing him ahead by over 30 points amid strong fundraising and low district Democratic performance historically. No major scandals or competitive public polling have emerged post-primaries, reinforcing trader confidence in his retention. Realistic challenges include a national Democratic wave boosting turnout, Thompson health issues, or late-breaking controversy, though these remain low-probability given the district's base rates for GOP dominance.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the SC-01 House race strongly favors the Republican Party at 79%, reflecting the district's solid Republican tilt (Cook PVI R+12) and incumbent Nancy Mace's commanding primary win over challenger Gregorie in June, where she secured 57% of the vote. eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Republican Party at 88.5% implied probability to win South Carolina's 2nd Congressional District House seat, driven by the district's strong GOP lean (Cook PVI R+12) and incumbent Rep. Joe Wilson's longstanding hold since 2001, including a dominant 96% primary victory on June 11. The Democratic nominee, Nathan Carson, advanced narrowly in his primary (52%), but faces historical GOP margins exceeding 30 points and limited fundraising. Candidate-specific markets show tight pricing around A (49.5%), B (50%), and Other (50.5%), reflecting some uncertainty over exact winner amid no recent polls or scandals. The November 5 general election remains the key catalyst.

eventMetadata.context_description: South Carolina's 3rd Congressional District, with its R+17 partisan voting index, drives the 92% trader consensus for a Republican House election win, bolstered by GOP nominee Sheri Biggs' decisive June primary victory after incumbent Jeff Duncan's retirement announcement. Recent Winthrop University polling showed Biggs leading Democrat Byron Smythe 54%-28%, aligning with nonpartisan forecasts rating the seat safe Republican amid limited Democratic infrastructure in the Upstate region. Early voting has begun without major catalysts, reflecting historical GOP dominance in similar districts. Realistic challenges include a sudden Biggs scandal or overwhelming national Democratic turnout, though base rates from past cycles suggest low probability of flipping.
